bites.title: MBO: Management by Objectives
bites.concept: MBO is a strategic management model that aims to improve organizational performance by clearly defining objectives that are agreed to by both management and employees. It involves a participative process of setting goals and tracking progress periodically.
bites.example: A bank manager and a loan officer agree on a specific target of 5 new high-value corporate loans per quarter.

check_question.question: Who is credited with popularizing the concept of 'Management by Objectives' (MBO)?
check_question.explanation: Peter Drucker first outlined the MBO process in his 1954 book 'The Practice of Management'.

bites.title: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
bites.concept: CAPM is used to determine the theoretically required rate of return of an asset, to make decisions about adding assets to a well-diversified portfolio. it considers the asset's sensitivity to non-diversifiable risk (Beta).
bites.example: If the risk-free rate is 7%, market return is 12%, and Beta is 1.2, then the required return = 7% + 1.2 * (12% - 7%) = 13%.

check_question.question: Calculate the Cost of Equity using CAPM: Risk-free rate (Rf) = 6%, Beta (β) = 1.5, Market Return (Rm) = 10%.

bites.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Mergers
bites.concept: A 'Horizontal Merger' occurs between companies in the same industry and at the same stage of production (e.g., two banks). A 'Vertical Merger' occurs between companies at different stages of the same supply chain (e.g., a bank and its software provider).
bites.example: HDFC Bank merging with HDFC is a horizontal merger (both in finance).

check_question.question: A merger between a manufacturer of automobile tyres and a manufacturer of automobiles is an example of:
check_question.explanation: The tyre manufacturer serves as a supplier to the automobile manufacturer, placing them at different stages of the supply chain.
